stem: Using spherical coordinates, find the volume of the spherical shell \( [[a]] \le \sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2} \le [[b]] \) (the region between the spheres of radius \([[a]]\) and \([[b]]\) centered at the origin).
distractor_rationales: This is the volume of the whole outer ball, forgetting to subtract the inner ball.
This adds the two radii cubed instead of subtracting them.
This subtracts the radii first and then cubes, which is not \(b^3-a^3\).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A solid (an 'ice-cream cone') is bounded above by the sphere \( \rho = [[R]] \) and below by the cone \( \phi = \pi/[[n]] \) (measuring \(\phi\) from the positive \(z\)-axis). Using spherical coordinates, find the volume of the solid.
distractor_rationales: This is the volume of the entire ball, forgetting the cone restricts \(\phi\) to \([0,\pi/[[n]]]\).
This uses \(\cos(\phi_0)\) alone instead of \(1-\cos(\phi_0)\), dropping the constant term from the \(\phi\)-antiderivative.
This uses the wedge-fraction formula (dividing by \([[n]]\)) as if the restriction were on \(\theta\) rather than \(\phi\).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A solid wedge is cut from a ball of radius \([[R]]\) centered at the origin by two half-planes through the \(z\)-axis, taking \( 0 \le \theta \le 2\pi/[[n]] \) (i.e. \(\tfrac{1}{[[n]]}\) of a full revolution, with \(\phi\) and \(\rho\) unrestricted). Using spherical coordinates, find the volume of the wedge.
solution: With \(dV=\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta\), the volume is \( \int_{0}^{2\pi/[[n]]}\int_{0}^{\pi}\int_{0}^{[[R]]} \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{[[n]]}\cdot 2\cdot\dfrac{[[R]]^3}{3} = \dfrac{4\pi[[R]]^3}{3[[n]]} = [[answer]] \), exactly \(\tfrac1{[[n]]}\) of the full ball's volume.
distractor_rationales: This uses the volume of the full ball, forgetting to restrict to the \(\tfrac1{[[n]]}\)-wedge.
This has an extra stray factor of \(\tfrac12\) from double-counting the wedge fraction.
This uses \(2\pi/3\) instead of \(4\pi/3\) for the full-ball coefficient, dropping a factor of \(2\).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A solid lies inside the ball \( \rho \le [[R]] \) and between the two cones \( \phi = \pi/[[n1]] \) and \( \phi = 2\pi/[[n2]] \) (measuring \(\phi\) from the positive \(z\)-axis, with \(\pi/[[n1]] < 2\pi/[[n2]]\)). Using spherical coordinates, find the volume of the solid.
distractor_rationales: This is the volume of the whole ball, ignoring the cone restrictions on \(\phi\) entirely.
This adds the two cosines instead of subtracting them (a sign error).
This uses the raw angle difference \(\phi_2-\phi_1\) in place of \(\cos\phi_1-\cos\phi_2\), forgetting the \(\sin\phi\) Jacobian.
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A right circular cone has its apex at the origin, opens upward along the positive \(z\)-axis with half-angle \(\phi_0=\pi/[[n]]\), and is capped by the horizontal plane \(z=[[H]]\). Using spherical coordinates (with \(\rho\) ranging up to where the ray meets the plane \(z=[[H]]\), i.e. \(\rho = [[H]]/\cos\phi\)), find the volume of the cone.
solution: With \(dV=\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta\) and the ray bounded by the plane at \(\rho=[[H]]/\cos\phi\), \( \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi/[[n]]}\int_0^{[[H]]/\cos\phi} \rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta = [[answer]] \), matching the elementary cone-volume formula \(\tfrac13\pi r^2 [[H]]\) with base radius \(r=[[H]]\tan(\pi/[[n]])\).
distractor_rationales: This is the volume of a full sphere of radius \([[H]]\), an unrelated formula.
This drops the required factor of \(\tfrac13\) from the cone-volume formula.
This uses the ice-cream-cone formula (sphere cap) instead of the flat-plane cap appropriate here.
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.

stem: A solid ball of radius \([[R]]\) centered at the origin has mass density \( \delta(x,y,z) = [[k]]\sqrt{x^2+y^2+z^2} \) (proportional to the distance from the center). Using spherical coordinates, find the total mass of the ball.
solution: In spherical coordinates \(\delta=[[k]]\rho\) and \(dV=\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta\), so the mass is \( \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi}\int_0^{[[R]]} [[k]]\rho\cdot\rho^2\sin\phi\,d\rho\,d\phi\,d\theta = 2\pi\cdot 2\cdot [[k]]\dfrac{[[R]]^4}{4} = \pi [[k]] [[R]]^4 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the volume formula for a uniform-density ball, ignoring the density factor \(\rho\) entirely.
This drops a factor of \(2\) from the \(\phi\)-integral (as if \(\int_0^\pi \sin\phi\,d\phi=1\) instead of \(2\)).
This uses \(\rho^5\) instead of \(\rho^4\) in the radial antiderivative, an extra power of \(\rho\).
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.
